problem_statement.summary: Data analysts cannot iterate quickly on reports because queries on large datasets take 30+ seconds.
problem_statement.current_state: Analysts run queries against production database directly. Queries on datasets with 1M+ rows take 30-60 seconds. Analysts must wait between iterations, breaking their flow and reducing productivity.
problem_statement.desired_state: Analysts can run queries on large datasets with sub-5-second response times, enabling rapid iteration on reports and analyses.
problem_statement.impact: Analysts spend approximately 2 hours per day waiting for queries. With 10 analysts, this represents 20 person-hours daily of lost productivity. Additionally, slow queries discourage exploratory analysis.

target_users.name: Data Analyst
target_users.description: Business analysts who create reports and dashboards for stakeholders. Moderately technical, comfortable with SQL but not programming.
needs: Access historical data for trend analysis
pain_points: Hesitant to run complex queries due to time cost

target_users.name: Data Engineer
target_users.description: Engineers who build and maintain data pipelines. Highly technical, programming expertise.
pain_points: Troubleshooting is slow due to query times

success_metrics.name: Query Response Time
success_metrics.description: 95th percentile response time for queries on 1M+ row datasets
success_metrics.measurement_method: Query latency monitoring via APM tool

success_metrics.name: Analyst Productivity
success_metrics.description: Number of reports/analyses produced per analyst per week
success_metrics.measurement_method: Count of published reports in analytics platform

success_metrics.name: Query Volume
success_metrics.description: Number of queries run per day (proxy for willingness to explore)

differentiators: Optimized for our specific data model

high_level_requirements.description: Implement query result caching for frequently run queries
high_level_requirements.rationale: Most analyst queries are repeated with minor variations. Caching eliminates redundant computation.

high_level_requirements.description: Create pre-aggregated materialized views for common report patterns
high_level_requirements.rationale: Common aggregations (daily summaries, weekly rollups) can be pre-computed to eliminate real-time aggregation.

high_level_requirements.description: Add query performance monitoring and alerting
high_level_requirements.rationale: Need visibility into query patterns to identify optimization opportunities and detect regressions.

high_level_requirements.description: Provide query optimization suggestions to analysts
high_level_requirements.rationale: Help analysts write more efficient queries through automated feedback.

constraints.description: Must use existing PostgreSQL database infrastructure
constraints.rationale: Operations team only has expertise in PostgreSQL. Cannot introduce new database technology.
Other engineers allocated to customer-facing features.

constraints.description: Must be ready for Q2 board presentation
constraints.rationale: CEO wants to demonstrate productivity improvements to the board.

non_goals: Real-time streaming analytics (batch processing is sufficient)
Self-service dashboard builder (analysts use existing tools)
Machine learning-based query optimization (manual tuning for now)
assumptions: Existing database hardware can handle additional caching load
Analysts are willing to adapt query patterns for better performance
Query patterns are predictable enough for effective caching
Data freshness requirements allow for up to 1-hour staleness in cached results

open_questions.question: What is the acceptable staleness for cached query results?
open_questions.context: Some reports may need real-time data while others can tolerate delay.
open_questions.impact: Affects cache invalidation strategy and complexity.
open_questions.answer: Up to 1 hour staleness is acceptable for most reports. Real-time reports will be tagged and excluded from caching.
